root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

the algebraic theory V: connections

Table of contents

  1. The natural connection on a manifold
  2. General connections
  3. Connections on pullbacks
  4. Differentiating sections along curves
  5. Connections on direct sums
  6. Connections on homs
  7. The category of modules with connections
  8. Connections on tensor products

Prerequisites

The natural connection on a manifold

Before we begin, we need to remember that if $(M,\sco)$ is a smooth manifold, then the structure sheaf $\sco$ is an algebra over the constant sheaf $\underline{\bbr}_M$ with value $\bbr$. If there is no ambiguity about the manifold under consideration, we will write $\underline{\bbr}$ in place of $\underline{\bbr}_M$. This extra structure on $\sco$ is extremely important in this post, because it allows us to encode $\bbr$-linearity at the level of sheaves.

Now, in order to motivate the definition of a connection on a general $\sco$-module $\cale$, we will examine the special case of a connection on the structure sheaf $\sco$:

Definition. Let $(M,\sco)$ be a manifold with tangent sheaf $\calx$. We define the natural connection on the manifold to be the $\underline{\bbr}$-linear map

\begin{equation}\notag D: \calx \otimes_\const \sco \to \sco, \quad X \otimes f \mapsto D_Xf, \end{equation}

where

\begin{equation}\notag D_Xf(p) = df_p(X_p). \end{equation}

So, the natural connection is nothing but the differential in disguise (and fancy language). It is an instructive exercise to prove to yourself that $D$ really is a well-defined $\const$-linear map. In particular, given an open subset $U$ of $M$, do you see why the induced map on local sections

\begin{equation}\notag D: \calx(U) \otimes_{\const (U)} \sco(U) \to \sco(U) \end{equation}

is $\const(U)$-linear? Once you justify this, you’ll also need to prove that $D$ commutes with restrictions, so that, given open sets $U\subseteq V$, the diagram

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ \color{white} \calx(V) \otimes_{\underline{\bbr}(V)} \sco(V) \ar@[white][d]_{\color{white} \text{res}} \ar@[white][r]^-{\color{white}D} & \color{white} \sco(V) \ar@[white][d]^{\color{white}\text{res}} \\ \color{white} \calx(U) \otimes_{\const (U)} \sco(U) \ar@[white][r]_-{\color{white}D} & \color{white} \sco(U)} \end{xy} \end{equation}

commutes. But, as you’ll see yourself, commutativity of this diagram crucially depends on the fact that differentials are local objects, in the sense that $df_p(X_p)$ may be computed on any open neighborhood of $p$.

Exercise. So, if you haven’t already, prove that the natural connection $D$ is a well-defined $\const$-linear map.

Now, there are two important properties that the natural connection $D$ possess that will be abstracted and become part of our definition of connections on general $\sco$-modules. Here’s the first:

Theorem (Natural connections depend $\sco$-linearly on vector fields). Let $D$ be the natural connection on a manifold $(M,\sco)$ with tangent sheaf $\calx$. Then

\begin{equation}\notag D: \calx \otimes_\const \sco \to \sco \end{equation}

is $\sco$-linear on the first tensor factor, in the sense that the diagram

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ \color{white}\sco \otimes \calx \otimes \sco \ar@[white][d]_{\color{white}\text{mult}\otimes \id} \ar@[white][r]^-{\color{white}\id \otimes D} & \color{white} \sco \otimes \calo \ar@[white][d]^{\color{white}\text{mult}} \\ \color{white} \calx \otimes \calo \ar@[white][r]_{\color{white}D} & \color{white} \sco} \end{xy} \end{equation}

commutes, where I’ve written $\otimes$ in place of $\otimes_\const$.

We can test for commutativity of the diagram locally on stalks. So, choose a point $p\in M$, and let $f,g\in \sco_p$ and $X\in \calx_p$. Then

\begin{equation}\notag \left(D\circ (\text{mult} \otimes \id)\right)(g \otimes X \otimes f) = D_{gX}f \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation}\notag \left(\text{mult} \circ (\id \otimes D)\right) = gD_Xf. \end{equation}

Now, for all $q\in M$ sufficiently close to $p$, we have

\begin{equation}\notag D_{gX}f(q) = df_q(g(q)X|_q) = g(q)df_q(X|_q) = (gD_Xf)(q). \end{equation}

Thus, the diagram commutes, as claimed. Q.E.D.

The second important property of natural connections is:

Theorem (Natural connections are derivations of functions). Let $D$ be the natural connection on a manifold $(M,\sco)$ with tangent sheaf $\calx$. Then

\begin{equation}\notag D: \calx \otimes_\const \sco \to \sco \end{equation}

is a derivation on the second tensor factor, in the sense that the diagram

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix@C=1in{ \color{white} \calx \otimes \sco \otimes \sco \ar@[white][r]^{\color{white}\cong} \ar@[white][d]_{\color{white}\id \otimes \text{mult}} & \color{white} \sco \otimes \calx \otimes \sco \ar@[white][r]^-{\color{white} (D \circ \text{tw}) \otimes \id + \id \otimes D} & \color{white} \sco \otimes \sco \ar@[white][d]^{\color{white}\text{mult}} \\ \color{white} \calx \otimes \sco \ar@[white][rr]_{\color{white}D} && \color{white} \sco} \end{xy} \end{equation}

commutes, where

\begin{equation}\notag \text{tw}: \calx \otimes \sco \to \sco \otimes \calx \end{equation}

denotes the natural “twisting map” (i.e., the natural commutativity isomorphism of the tensor product).

Again, we can test for commutativity of the diagram locally on stalks. Let $p\in M$ be a point, and $f,g\in \sco_p$ and $X\in \calx_p$. Starting with the tensor product

\begin{equation}\notag X \otimes g \otimes f \end{equation}

in the upper-left hand corner of the diagram and proceeding to the lower-right corner by first going along the top horizontal composite map, we get

\begin{equation}\notag X \otimes g \otimes f \mapsto (D_Xg)f + gD_Xf. \end{equation}

Along the other path to the lower-right corner, we get

\begin{equation}\notag X \otimes g \otimes f \mapsto D_X(gf). \end{equation}

Now, for all $q\in M$ sufficiently close to $p$, we have

\begin{equation}\notag D_X(gf)(q) = d(gf)_q(X|_q) = dg_q(X|_q)f(q) + g(q) df_q(X|_q) = \left((D_Xg)f + gD_Xf\right)(q). \end{equation}

Thus

\begin{equation}\label{leibniz-eqn} D_X(gf) = (D_Xg)f + gD_Xf, \end{equation}

as desired. Q.E.D.

Equation \eqref{leibniz-eqn} is highlighted because it is important enough to have a name: It is called the Leibniz rule, or the product rule.

While we are not done with our study of the natural connection on a manifold, you have essentially seen all that you need in order to motivate the definition of a connection on a general $\sco$-module. If you’d like, scroll down and take a peek at the definition before continuing with this section. It should look familiar!

The local nature of the natural connection $D$ may be exploited to give an equivalent structure on the manifold. Admittedly, this new structure isn’t so interesting for the natural connection $D$, but it will inspire a similar construction for general connections which will prove to be highly useful.

Definition. Let $(M,\sco)$ be a manifold with tangent sheaf $\calx$. We define the natural field of directional derivatives on $M$ to be the collection of $\bbr$-linear maps

\begin{equation}\notag D^p : T_p(M) \otimes_\bbr \sco_p \to \bbr, \quad v \otimes f \mapsto D^p_v f \defeq df_p(v), \end{equation}

one for each $p\in M$.

So, we have the natural connection $D$ and the natural field of directional derivatives $\{D^p\}_{p\in M}$. Since both are defined through differentials, it is perhaps not surprising that these are equivalent structures on the manifold. However, as I will now show, the directional derivatives $D^p$ may actually be recovered from the natural connection $D$, not based on their mutual pre-existing definitions in terms of differentials, but (miraculously!) through the $\sco$-linearity of $D$ on its first tensor factor.

In fact, as you will see, $\sco$-linearity is the crucial ingredient in the following preliminary fact:

Theorem (Natural connections depend pointwise on vector fields). Let $D$ be the natural connection on a manifold $(M,\sco)$ with tangent sheaf $\calx$. If $X,Y\in \calx(U)$ are two vector fields for which $X_p = Y_p$ at a point $p\in U$, then

\begin{equation}\notag D_Xf(p) = D_Yf(p) \end{equation}

for all $f\in \sco(U)$.

Contemplate carefully the title of this theorem:

“Natural connections depend pointwise on vector fields.”

For comparison, the title of the theorem about $\sco$-linearity was:

“Natural connections depend $\sco$-linearly on vector fields.”

In fact, $\sco$-linear dependence is equivalent to pointwise and $\const$-linear dependence. The theorem as stated is one half of this equivalence; you might try to find a proof of the converse on your own.

The argument is standard and is contained in nearly every book on differential geometry. I will leave it to you to show that it suffices to prove

\begin{equation}\notag D_Xf(p) = 0 \end{equation}

for all $f\in \sco(U)$, provided that $X_p=0$. We may resolve $X$ in terms of components in a local coordinate frame on an open neighborhood $V$ of $p$ as

\begin{equation}\notag X = X^i \bd_i. \end{equation}

By shrinking $V$ if needed, we may choose a bump function $h\in \sco(U)$ with support in $V$ and $h(p)=1$. But then each $hX^i$ may be considered a smooth function on all of $U$, and likewise each $h\bd_i$ is a smooth vector field on $U$. Then

\begin{equation}\notag h^2 D_Xf = D_{h^2X}f = hX^i D_{h \bd_i}f, \end{equation}

and by evaluating at $p$ we get

\begin{equation}\notag D_Xf(p) = h(p)^2 D_Xf(p) = h(p)X^i(p) D_{h \bd_i}f(p) = 0, \end{equation}

since $X^i(p)=0$ for each $i$. Q.E.D.

Now, here’s the result—precisely stated—that shows the natural field of directional derivatives may be recovered from the natural connection:

Theorem (Natural connections determine natural directional derivatives). Let $D$ be the natural connection on a manifold $(M,\sco)$ with tangent sheaf $\calx$. Given a point $p\in M$, a function $f\in \sco_p$, and a vector $T_p(M)$, suppose that we define

\begin{equation}\notag D^p_vf = D_{X_p}f(p), \end{equation}

where $X$ is a vector field defined in an open neighborhood of $p$ with $X_p = v$. Then $D^p_vf$ does not depend on the choice of $X$, and coincides with the natural directional derivative as defined above.

It is well known that such vector fields $X$ always exist. The fact that $D^p_vf$ does not depend on the choice of $X$ is exactly the conclusion of the previous theorem (which was a consequence of $\sco$-linearity!), so there’s nothing really to prove.

General connections

You now have everything that you need in order to appreciate the following definition, one of the most important in all of differential geometry.

Definition. Let $(M,\sco)$ be a manifold with tangent sheaf $\calx$ and natural connection $D$, and let $\cale$ be an $\sco$-module. A connection on $\cale$ is an $\underline{\bbr}$-linear morphism

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla : \calx \otimes_{\underline{\bbr}} \cale \to \cale, \quad X \otimes s \mapsto \nabla_Xs, \end{equation}

which is $\sco$-linear on the first tensor factor and a derivation on the second. Precisely, this means that the two diagrams

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ \color{white}\sco \otimes \calx \otimes \cale \ar@[white][d]_{\color{white}\text{mult}\otimes \id} \ar@[white][r]^-{\color{white}\id \otimes \nabla} & \color{white} \sco \otimes \cale \ar@[white][d]^{\color{white}\text{mult}} \\ \color{white} \calx \otimes \cale \ar@[white][r]_{\color{white}\nabla} & \color{white} \cale} \end{xy} \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix@C=1in{ \color{white} \calx \otimes \cale \otimes \sco \ar@[white][r]^{\color{white}\cong} \ar@[white][d]_{\color{white}\id \otimes \text{mult}} & \color{white} \sco \otimes \calx \otimes \cale \ar@[white][r]^-{\color{white} (D \otimes \id)\circ \text{tw} + \id \otimes \nabla} & \color{white} \sco \otimes \cale \ar@[white][d]^{\color{white}\text{mult}} \\ \color{white} \calx \otimes \cale \ar@[white][rr]_{\color{white}\nabla} && \color{white} \cale} \end{xy} \end{equation}

both commute, where

\begin{equation}\notag \text{tw}: \sco \otimes \calx \to \calx \otimes \sco \end{equation}

denotes the natural “twisting map” (i.e., the natural commutativity isomorphism of the tensor product).

In this language, we would say that the natural connection $D$ is a connection on the structure sheaf $\sco$ (as an $\sco$-module). So, you already know of an example!

Our study of natural connections suggests that connections $\nabla$ on $\cale$ should provide directional derivatives which act on sections of $\cale$. Indeed, they do! What follows is the relevant definition to make this notion precise, adapted from the previous section. In it, remember that $\cale_p$ and $E_p$ are different objects; the former is the stalk of $\cale$ at $p$, while the latter is the fiber of $\cale$ at $p$. Given a section $s\in \cale(U)$ and a point $p\in U$, its value in the fiber is denoted $s_p\in E_p$.

Definition. Let $(M,\sco)$ be a manifold with tangent sheaf $\calx$ and let $\cale$ be an $\sco$-module. We define a field of directional derivatives on $\cale$ to be a collection of $\bbr$-linear maps

\begin{equation}\label{field-eqn} \nabla^p : T_p(M) \otimes_\bbr \cale_p \to E_p, \quad v \otimes s \mapsto \nabla^p_v s, \end{equation}

one for each $p\in M$, such that

\begin{equation}\label{leib-eqn} \nabla^p_v(fs) = df_p(v) s_p + f(p) \nabla_v^ps \end{equation}

for all $v\in T_p(M)$, $ s \in \cale_p$, and $f\in C^{\infty}_{M,p}$.

The field \eqref{field-eqn} is said to be smooth if, for all open sets $U\subseteq M$, all smooth vector fields $X\in \calx(U)$, and all sections $s \in \cale(U)$, the induced section

\begin{equation}\notag p\mapsto \nabla_{X_p}^p s \in E_p \end{equation}

is in $\cale(U)$.

Notice that the Leibniz rule \eqref{leib-eqn} is built into the definition, which wasn’t required for the natural field of directional derivatives in the last section.

The main result of this section is to prove that every connection $\nabla$ on a locally free $\sco$-module $\cale$ induces a smooth field of directional derivatives on $\cale$, and conversely, every such field induces a connection. These correspondences between connections and directional derivatives are inverses of each other, so if you perform one and then the other, you obtain the original object.

In order to prove this, we need the following important (and familiar!) result. Notice that the $\sco$-module is assumed to be locally free.

Theorem (Connections depend pointwise on vector fields). Let $(M,\sco)$ be a manifold with tangent sheaf $\calx$ and let $\nabla$ be a connection on a locally free $\sco$-module $\cale$. If $X,Y\in \calx(U)$ are two vector fields for which $X_p = Y_p$ at a point $p\in U$, then

\begin{equation}\notag (\nabla_Xs)_p = (\nabla_Ys)_p \end{equation}

for all sections $s\in \cale(U)$.

Exercise. Prove the theorem.

Here’s the main theorem:

Theorem (Connections and directional derivatives are equivalent). Let $(M,\sco)$ be a manifold with tangent sheaf $\calx$, and let $\cale$ be a locally free $\sco$-module.

  1. Let $\nabla^p$ be a smooth field of directional derivatives on $\cale$. For each open set $U\subseteq M$, vector field $X\in \calx(U)$, and section $s\in \cale(U)$, suppose that we define \begin{equation}\notag (\nabla_Xs)_p = \nabla^p_{X_p} s \end{equation} for all $p\in U$. Then the induced map \begin{equation}\notag \nabla : \calx \otimes_\const \cale \to \cale, \quad X\otimes s \mapsto \nabla_X s, \end{equation} is a connection on $\cale$.
  2. Let $\nabla$ be a connection on $\cale$. For each point $p\in M$, vector $v\in T_p(M)$, and section $s \in \cale_p$, suppose that we define \begin{equation}\notag \nabla^p_vs = (\nabla_{X}s)_p \end{equation} where $X$ is a vector field on an open neighborhood of $p$ with $X_p=v$. Then $\nabla^p_vs$ does not depend on the choice of $p$, and the induced maps \begin{equation}\notag \nabla^p : T_p(M) \otimes_\bbr \cale_p \to E_p, \quad v \otimes s \mapsto \nabla^p_v s, \end{equation} yield a smooth field of directional derivatives on $\cale$.

Exercise. The proof amounts to checking all of the details. There’s nothing too hard or tricky. Try it out on your own!

One of the main uses of connections is to define classes of sections of $\sco$-modules which are “constant” in a sense. This will be very important later, when we use Levi-Civita connections on tangent sheaves of riemannian manifolds to define the important parallel transport operation.

Definition. Let $(M,\sco)$ be a manifold with tangent sheaf $\calx$ and let $\cale$ be an $\sco$-module with connection $\nabla$. Then a section $s\in \cale(U)$ is called horizontal (relative to $\nabla$) if $\nabla_Xs=0$ for all $X\in \calx(U)$.

The terminology is motivated by the natural connection $D$ on $\bbr$ and connections on other vector bundles for which there is a natural “vertical orientation” with the total space stacked on top of the base space. In this case, if we are given a smooth function $f:\bbr \to \bbr$ such that $D_Xf=0$ for all vector fields of $X$, then the graph of $f$ is, indeed, horizontal.

We may compare two $\sco$-modules by constructing an $\sco$-linear map between them; if those $\sco$-modules happen to have connections, then we may compare them using the following special types of $\sco$-linear maps:

Definition. Let $(M,\sco)$ be a manifold with tangent sheaf $\calx$, and let $\cale$ and $\cale’$ be two $\sco$-modules with connections $\nabla$ and $\nabla’$, respectively. An $\sco$-linear map

\begin{equation}\notag \theta : \cale \to \cale’ \end{equation}

is called a morphism of modules with connections if the diagram

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ \color{white} \calx \otimes_\const \cale \ar@[white][r]^-{\color{white}\nabla} \ar@[white][d]_{\color{white}\id \otimes \theta} & \color{white} \cale \ar@[white][d]^{\color{white}\theta} \\ \color{white} \calx \otimes_\const \cale’ \ar@[white][r]_-{\color{white}\nabla’} & \color{white} \cale’} \end{xy} \end{equation}

commutes.

So far, I have eschewed coordinates, typical of a mathematician with an inclination toward intricate abstract algebraic structures. :) However, this doesn’t mean that computations in local coordinates aren’t important! To help facilitate them, we introduce the following objects:

Definition. Let $(M,\sco)$ be a manifold with tangent sheaf $\calx$ and let $\cale$ be a locally free $\sco$-module with connection $\nabla$. Suppose that $U\subseteq M$ is an open subset over which $M$ has a coordinate frame

\begin{equation}\label{coord-eqn} \bd_1,\ldots,\bd_n \in \calx(U), \end{equation}

and suppose that $\cale$ trivializes over $U$ with local frame

\begin{equation}\label{framey-eqn} e_1,\ldots, e_r \in \cale(U). \end{equation}

Then for all $i=1,\ldots,n$ and $j=1,\ldots,r$ we have

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla_{\bd_i}e_j = \Gamma^k_{ij} e_k, \end{equation}

where the functions $\Gamma_{ij}^k \in \sco(U)$ are called the connection coefficients of $\nabla$ relative to the frames \eqref{coord-eqn} and \eqref{framey-eqn}.

When $\nabla$ is the Levi-Civita connection on a riemannian manifold, the connection coefficients $\Gamma^k_{ij}$ are classically called Christoffel symbols.

The importance of the connection coefficients comes from the local expressions of connections that are obtainable using them:

Theorem. Let $(M,\sco)$ be a manifold with tangent sheaf $\calx$ and let $\cale$ be a locally free $\sco$-module with connection $\nabla$. Let $\Gamma^k_{ij}$ be the connection coefficients over an open set $U$ relative to a coordinate frame $\bd_i$ of $\calx(U)$ and a frame $e_j$ of $\cale(U)$. Given a vector field $X\in \calx(U)$ and a section $s\in \cale(U)$, we have

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla_X s = \left(D_Xs^k + X^i s^j\Gamma^k_{ij}\right)e_k. \end{equation}

In particular, if the connection coefficients all vanish on $U$, then $\nabla_Xs$ is computed simply by taking component-wise derivatives.

Connections on pullbacks

Our first goal is to use the correspondence between connections and directional derivatives to define pullback connections. It turns out that attempting to construct these connections from the definition of a connection is quite fussy. However, with directional derivatives, it becomes a breeze.

Here’s the situation. Suppose that we have a morphism $\alpha:(N,\sco_N) \to (M,\sco_M)$ of manifolds and a locally free $\sco_M$-module $\cale$ with connection $\nabla$. In the previous post, we constructed the pullback module $\alpha^\ast \cale$, which is locally free over $\sco_N$. We ask: Is it possible to construct a pullback connection on $\alpha^\ast \cale$?

As I mentioned, the answer is yes, and the pullback connection is often denoted $\alpha^\ast \nabla$. However, despite what the notation might suggest, it is not the pullback of the map

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla: \calx_M \otimes_{\const_M} \cale \to \cale \end{equation}

under the pullback functor

\begin{equation}\notag \alpha^\ast: \Mod{\sco_M} \to \Mod{\sco_N}. \end{equation}

This is because $\nabla$ is not a morphism in the category $\Mod{\sco_M}$; remember, it is only $\const_M$-linear, not $\sco_M$-linear!

Here’s our construction:

Theorem. Let $\alpha:(N,\sco_N) \to (M,\sco_M)$ be a morphism of manifolds and let $\cale$ be a locally free $\sco_M$-module with connection $\nabla$. There exists a unique connection

\begin{equation}\notag \alpha^\ast\nabla: \calx_N \otimes_{\const_N} \alpha^\ast \cale \to \alpha^\ast \cale \end{equation}

whose directional derivatives $\alpha^\ast\nabla^q$ satisfy the relations

\begin{equation}\label{defining-eqn} \alpha^\ast\nabla^q_w(\alpha^\ast s) = \nabla^v_ps \end{equation}

for all $w\in T_q(N)$ and $s \in \cale_p$, where $p = \alpha(q)$ and $v = \alpha_{\ast,q}(w)$.

Suppose that such a connection $\alpha^\ast \nabla$ exists, satisfying \eqref{defining-eqn}. Then, since the natural map

\begin{equation}\label{zeroth-eqn} \sco_{N,q} \otimes_{\sco_{M,p}} \cale_p \to (\alpha \cale)_q, \quad g \otimes s \mapsto g \alpha^\ast s, \end{equation}

is an isomorphism, the action of $\alpha^\ast \nabla^q_w$ on the stalk $(\alpha \cale)_q$ is determined by its action on (germs of) sections of the form $g\alpha^\ast s$. But, by the Leibniz rule, we have

\begin{equation}\label{extend-eqn} \alpha^\ast\nabla^q_w(g \alpha^\ast s) = dg_q(w) s_p + g(q) \nabla^p_vs \end{equation}

where $p = \alpha(q)$ and $v = \alpha_{\ast,q}(w)$. Thus, if the connection $\alpha^\ast \nabla$ exists, then it is uniquely determined by $\nabla$.

To establish existence of $\alpha^\ast \nabla$, we shall construct its directional derivatives $\alpha^\ast \nabla^q$. The first step is to consider the isomorphism

\begin{equation}\label{first-eqn} T_q(N) \otimes_\bbr (\alpha^\ast \cale)_q \xrightarrow{\cong} T_q(N) \otimes_\bbr ( \sco_{N,q} \otimes_{\sco_{M,p}} \cale_p) \end{equation}

induced by the natural map \eqref{zeroth-eqn} above. Now, we have two maps

\begin{equation}\label{second-eqn} (T_q(N) \otimes_\bbr \sco_{N,q} ) \otimes_{\sco_{M,p}} \cale_p \xrightarrow{d \otimes \dev_p} \bbr \otimes_{\sco_{M,p}} E_p \to E_p \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation}\label{third-eqn} \sco_{N,q} \otimes_{\sco_{M,p}} ( T_q(N) \otimes_\bbr \cale_p ) \xrightarrow{\id \otimes \alpha_{\ast,q} \otimes \id} \sco_{N,q} \otimes_{\sco_{M,p}} ( T_p(M) \otimes_\bbr \cale_p ) \xrightarrow{\dev_q \otimes \nabla^p} \bbr \otimes_{\sco_{M,p}} E_p \to E_p. \end{equation}

Notice that the domains of \eqref{second-eqn} and \eqref{third-eqn} are naturally isomorphic to the codomain of the isomorphism \eqref{first-eqn} via natural associativity and commutativity isomorphisms of the tensor product. By composing \eqref{second-eqn} with \eqref{first-eqn}, we obtain one map

\begin{equation}\notag T_q(N) \otimes_\bbr (\alpha^\ast \cale)_q \to E_p, \end{equation}

and by composing \eqref{third-eqn} with \eqref{first-eqn}, we obtain a second. We then define

\begin{equation}\label{field1-eqn} \alpha^\ast\nabla^q: T_q(N) \otimes_\bbr (\alpha^\ast \cale)_q \to E_p \end{equation}

to be the sum of the two maps obtained in this manner. As you may easily check, the resulting map $\alpha^\ast \nabla^q_w$ satisfies the Leibniz rule for each $w\in T_q(N)$, and thus we have obtained a field of directional derivatives which will (after we establish smoothness) induce the connection $\alpha^\ast \nabla$. If you trace through this argument, you’ll notice that we have essentially defined the connection $\alpha^\ast\nabla$ by the formula \eqref{extend-eqn}.

All that remains is to show that the field of directional derivatives \eqref{field1-eqn} is smooth. For this, we fix an open set $V\subseteq N$, a smooth vector field $Y\in \calx_N(V)$, and a section $s\in \alpha^\ast\cale(V)$. We must then show that the mapping

\begin{equation}\notag q \mapsto \alpha^\ast \nabla^q_{Y_q}s \in E_p \end{equation}

defines a section in $\alpha^\ast\cale(V)$. But this is a purely local condition, so, by shrinking $V$ if needed, we may assume that $V$ is parametrized and that $\alpha(V) \subseteq U$, where $U$ is an open parametrizable subset of $M$ over which $\cale$ trivializes. If we choose a frame

\begin{equation}\label{frame-eqn} e_1,\ldots,e_r \in \cale(U), \end{equation}

then the pullbacks

\begin{equation}\notag \alpha^\ast e_1,\ldots,\alpha^\ast e_r \in \alpha^\ast\cale(V) \end{equation}

form a local frame for the pullback sheaf $\alpha^\ast \cale$. Thus, we may write

\begin{equation}\notag s = s^j \alpha^\ast e_j, \end{equation}

where $s^j \in \sco_N(V)$ are the component functions of $s$. In terms of the coordinate frame on $V$, suppose that we also write

\begin{equation}\notag Y = Y^l \bd_l. \end{equation}

Now, for each $q\in V$, I will leave it as a straightforward computation to prove that

\begin{equation}\notag \alpha^\ast \nabla^q_{Y_q}s = \left[Y^l(q) (\bd_l s^k)(q) + Y^l(q) s^j(q) (\bd_l\alpha^i)(q) \alpha^\ast\Gamma^k_{ij}(q) \right] \alpha^\ast e_k|_{q}, \end{equation}

where $\Gamma^k_{ij}$ are the connection coefficients of $\nabla$ relative to the frame \eqref{frame-eqn}. But all the terms in the brackets smoothly depend on $q$. Thus, the directional derivatives \eqref{field-eqn} are indeed smooth, and they induce the desired connection $\alpha^\ast \nabla$. Q.E.D.

Differentiating sections along curves

Definition. Let $(M,\sco)$ be a smooth manifold, let $\cale$ be a locally free $\sco$-module with connection $\nabla$, and let $\gamma: \bbr \to M$ be a smooth curve. Given a local section $s\in \gamma^\ast\cale(V)$ over an open set $V\subseteq \bbr$, we define the time derivative of $s$ to be the section $\dot{s} \in \gamma^\ast \cale(V)$ where

\begin{equation}\notag \dot{s}(t) = \gamma^\ast \nabla^t_1 s \end{equation}

for each $t\in V$.

If $\Gamma^k_{ij}$ are the connection coefficients of $\nabla$ relative to some smooth local frame $\{e_k\}$ of $\cale$, then we have

\begin{equation}\notag \dot{s} = \left( \dot{s}^k + \dot{\gamma}^is^j\gamma^\ast \Gamma^k_{ij}\right)\gamma^\ast e_k. \end{equation}

In particular, if the connection coefficients vanish, then $\dot{s}$ is given by the familiar formula

\begin{equation}\notag \dot{s} = \dot{s}^k \gamma^\ast e_k. \end{equation}
